questions.question: Define a sparse matrix. Explain the triplet (array of triples) representation with an example.
keyPoints: Definition: matrix where majority of elements are zero
Triplet stores (row, col, value) for each non-zero element
First entry stores dimensions and count of non-zero terms
Demonstrate with a sample 4x4 sparse matrix

questions.question: Explain the linked list (multilist/orthogonal list) representation of a sparse matrix with a diagram.
keyPoints: Node structure: row, col, value, next-in-row, next-in-col
Row header array and column header array
Each node belongs to two linked lists (row and column)
Diagram showing cross-linked structure
Advantages over array representation for dynamic operations

questions.question: Write an algorithm for the fast transpose of a sparse matrix stored in triplet representation. Analyze its time complexity.
keyPoints: Compute number of non-zero elements in each column
Determine starting position for each column in transposed array
Place each element directly in correct position
Time complexity: O(columns + non-zero terms)
Compare with simple transpose O(columns * non-zero terms)

questions.question: Write a function to add two sparse matrices represented using linked lists.
keyPoints: Traverse both matrices row by row
Merge elements with same row-column position by adding values
Copy elements unique to either matrix
Handle case where sum is zero (do not insert)
Time complexity: O(t1 + t2)

questions.question: Compare triplet representation and linked list representation of sparse matrices.
keyPoints: Triplet: simple array-based, fixed size, fast access by index
Linked list: dynamic, supports insertion/deletion efficiently
Triplet: poor for frequent modifications (shifting required)
Linked list: more memory per node (extra pointers)
Choose based on whether matrix is static or dynamic
